Children.Text: VeloxDev provides .NET developers with the complete architecture required to build interactive workflow editors.

### Adapter Library Out of the box, bound to GUI frameworks; for agent or serialization capabilities, install `VeloxDev.Core.Extension` separately.

| Platform | Package | NuGet |
|----------|---------|-------|
| WPF | `VeloxDev.WPF` | [![NuGet](https://img.shields.io/nuget/v/VeloxDev.WPF?color=4caf50&logo=nuget)](https://www.nuget.org/packages/VeloxDev.WPF/) |
| Avalonia | `VeloxDev.Avalonia` | [![NuGet](https://img.shields.io/nuget/v/VeloxDev.Avalonia?color=4caf50&logo=nuget)](https://www.nuget.org/packages/VeloxDev.Avalonia/) |
| WinUI | `VeloxDev.WinUI` | [![NuGet](https://img.shields.io/nuget/v/VeloxDev.WinUI?color=4caf50&logo=nuget)](https://www.nuget.org/packages/VeloxDev.WinUI/) |
| MAUI | `VeloxDev.MAUI` | [![NuGet](https://img.shields.io/nuget/v/VeloxDev.MAUI?color=4caf50&logo=nuget)](https://www.nuget.org/packages/VeloxDev.MAUI/) |
| WinForms | `VeloxDev.WinForms` | [![NuGet](https://img.shields.io/nuget/v/VeloxDev.WinForms?color=4caf50&logo=nuget)](https://www.nuget.org/packages/VeloxDev.WinForms/) |
| Razor | `VeloxDev.Razor` | [![NuGet](https://img.shields.io/nuget/v/VeloxDev.Razor?color=4caf50&logo=nuget)](https://www.nuget.org/packages/VeloxDev.Razor/) |

### Core Library Independent of GUI frameworks, optional components; their updates may outpace those of the adaptation libraries, and sometimes require separate installation/upgrades.

| Package | NuGet | Description |
|---------|-------|-------------|
| `VeloxDev.Core` | [![NuGet](https://img.shields.io/nuget/v/VeloxDev.Core?color=4caf50&logo=nuget)](https://www.nuget.org/packages/VeloxDev.Core/) | Workflow abstractions, MVVM generators, and runtime models — zero third-party dependencies |
| `VeloxDev.Core.Extension` | [![NuGet](https://img.shields.io/nuget/v/VeloxDev.Core.Extension?color=4caf50&logo=nuget)](https://www.nuget.org/packages/VeloxDev.Core.Extension/) | MAF-based Workflow Agent tools and additional runtime extensions |
By reviewing this chapter, you will quickly learn about historically significant version updates.
The first declared available version, on which this documentation is based; all subsequent version upgrades will be consolidated in this section.
Summary: Agent Construction Paradigm Upgrade & Additional Branch Node Creation Methods
I. Upgrade of the Agent Construction Paradigm

Now, agents can initiate permission confirmation dialogs and radio buttons—a significant milestone that marks your ability to orchestrate agent tasks in a more controlled manner. Meanwhile, the workspace now includes a new set of practical configuration methods, such as global language configuration and automatic workflow context scanning from assemblies.
II Routing nodes have greater extensibility
First, there is a brand-new interface definition: when you want to create a branch with multiple connectors using a custom type, you can implement the `ISlotProvider` interface.
Thus, you can easily implement a conditional provider for rendering multiple connectors at once.

Finally, when you want custom conditional routing inside a Node component, you can use the `CustomRouteSelector` defined earlier to generate it dynamically. Once defined, the agent will automatically understand the `CustomRouteSelector` construction based on serialization, and dynamically generate connectors through instantiation and passing of the object.
Focus on practical application and master how to use the project.
VeloxDev provides featured ViewModel completion and Command generation, compatible with mainstream MVVM frameworks in the community, and avoids redundant generation of infrastructure.

Children.Code: [VeloxProperty] private string name = string.Empty;
[VeloxProperty] public partial string Name { get; protected set; }

Children.Text: If the member derives from `INotifyCollectionChanged`, additional callback methods are provided. Specifically, when the value of a notifying property changes, subscription to and disposal of the callback methods are handled automatically internally. Note that partial properties cannot declare initial values at definition time; therefore, we recommend initializing these collections during construction.
The following code will all ultimately generate a `SaveCommand`, regardless of whether it carries the “Async” suffix.

No matter how you execute the command, the underlying mechanism is always waiting on a Task; therefore, in most cases you don’t need to worry about calling `Execute` instead of `ExecuteAsync` blocking the UI — they are nearly equivalent.

Children.Code:
SaveCommand.Execute(null);

await SaveCommand.ExecuteAsync(null);

Children.Text: Command cancellation is also available in two versions; here we demonstrate the awaitable version.
When you set `canValidate: true`, the `CanExecuteSaveCommand` callback is internally generated, and you can determine whether to allow command execution inside the callback function.

The UI can be manually notified to re-evaluate the command’s executability; at that time, the `parameter` is `null`. If the command is executed, the `parameter` is the argument received by the command.

Children.Code: SaveCommand.Notify();

Children.Text: You can listen for events such as the total number of currently executing tasks and perform exception handling; all these callback events carry a `CommandEventArgs` parameter.

.NET 5+ natively supports proxy-based aspect-oriented programming, meaning that dynamic interception and customization of property and method behavior can be achieved without modifying the type’s source code; VeloxDev provides配套 source code generation for this.
When configuring a proxy, there are five key parameters.
Use these parameters in the `SetProxy` method.

Cells: Parameters received at the entry point, e.g., the `value` keyword in properties or parameters retrieved during method invocation
Parameters returned in the previous lifecycle

Children.Text: Hooks only take effect when properties are accessed through a proxy; in actual business scenarios, you can choose as needed.
Frame loop behavior is common in scenarios such as physics simulation and state monitoring. Now, you can build it using an attribute.
You can create multiple groups based on the `channel` parameter and specify the refresh rate using the `fps` parameter.
Basic control can be implemented based on these static functions.

Children.Code:
MonoBehaviourManager.Start("game"); // Start

MonoBehaviourManager.Pause("game"); // Pause (not equivalent to Stop)

MonoBehaviourManager.Resume("game"); // Resume

await MonoBehaviourManager.StopAsync("game"); // Stop

Children.Text: You can modify the time scale and refresh rate at any time.

Children.Code:
MonoBehaviourManager.SetTimeScale(0.5f, "game"); // time scale

MonoBehaviourManager.SetTargetFPS(30, "game"); // frame rate

bool isRun = MonoBehaviourManager.IsRunning("game"); // Checks whether the "game" MonoBehaviour is currently running

bool isPaused = MonoBehaviourManager.IsPaused("game"); // Checks whether the "game" MonoBehaviour is currently paused

Cells: Time elapsed since the previous frame

Children.Text: Almost any GUI framework provides proprietary interfaces for handling animations, making the creation of interpolation-based animations somewhat dependent on the GUI’s syntactic design—and potentially subject to limitations. Take WPF as an example: standard animations must be built upon `DependencyProperty`. While this design choice facilitates internal performance optimization, it also sacrifices flexibility.

VeloxDev advocates compatibility and internally designs a complete abstraction layer for animations—from construction to execution—so that each GUI framework requires only minimal adaptation code, and ultimately animations operate on standard properties rather than any GUI-specific objects.
Theme — Vivid Theme Color Switching Implementation
You can use the same API to build animations in any supported GUI framework—we provide the most elegant approach to date, with strong emphasis on type safety and thread safety.
In the following example, you will create an animation that modifies the horizontal offset and fill color of a Rectangle element.
The animation process can be split into multiple segments and then connected sequentially.

Children.Code:
Transition<Rectangle>.Create()
    .Property()
    .Effect()
    .Then()  // Chain: execute the next animation immediately
    .Property()
    .Effect()
    .AwaitThen() // Chain: wait for a specified time before executing the next animation
    .Property()
    .Effect();

Children.Text: Use the following code to control the animation toggle.

Children.Code:
animation.Execute(rect); // Start the animation on the rect instance

Transition.Exit(rect); // Terminate

Children.Text: Animation startup is thread-safe; you need not worry about the following scenarios.

The `IInterpolable` interface allows types to participate directly in interpolation. By implementing this interface, your custom types can be used directly with the `Property` function when building animations in Fluent.
The `IEaseCalculator` interface allows you to customize easing effects.

Animations are based on interpolation between two states; the snapshot mechanism is one of its implementations. VeloxDev exposes this mechanism, so you can use it to create animations—or for state saving and restoration (used only in animation scenarios).

Children.Code:
// The essence is still animation; effects can be configured.
snapshot.Effect(TransitionEffects.Empty).Execute(view);

Children.Text: This is an extended capability derived from the animation system, enabling natural transitions between theme color palettes within the UI.
If you configure an interpolator, the following functions become available.
Of course, a non-animated transition mode is also provided to address potential performance issues in certain scenarios; this mode is supported at all times.

Workflow orchestration and automation are common in modern software—especially in business pipelines and device control flows. Today, you can elegantly build extensible, runtime workflows in .NET. Moreover, as AI advances, enabling agents to understand and manipulate in-memory objects is no longer far-fetched. What you need is creativity and approval—not extensive coding.
Avalonia / WPF / WinUI / MAUI / Winforms have all implemented the following effect.
Based on MVVM, with no GUI dependencies

You can render an interactive workflow orchestration view in your preferred GUI framework, or run orchestrated workflows on a headless server. VeloxDev.Core.Extension provides persistence services to support sharing the same data across these modes.
Spatial indexing and querying of nodes based on the Grid Hashing algorithm; measured response time is in the microsecond range.
The workflow framework has deeply integrated the Function Calling capability provided by MAF, enabling your agent to understand and manipulate workflows at runtime.
WorkflowBuilder provides source code generation capability; simply annotate this feature to implement the workflow component kernel without inheritance.

WorkflowHelper provides the logical code required by workflow components. It is injected into the view model during the generation phase and supports dynamic replacement, enabling a highly extensible architecture by overriding built-in Helper methods and subscribing to built-in Helper events.
A workflow is a graph structure containing four key components. This section demonstrates how to build the corresponding view models for these components.

Cells: Global container that stores all data for a single workflow
Node that hosts configuration and business logic execution
Connector that hosts connection relationships and channel direction constraints
Connection line that can access the source and destination Slot

Children.Text: Next, all view models will be built based on the following attributes: `$component` is the component type, and `$helper` is the injected business logic.

Children.Code: 【WorkflowBuilder.{$component}<{$helper}>】

Children.Text:
| Member                                             | Type                                                                                               | Description              |
| -------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------ |
| `VirtualLink`                                    | `IWorkflowLinkViewModel`                                                                         | Virtual connection during connection establishment |
| `Nodes`                                          | `ObservableCollection<IWorkflowNodeViewModel>`                                                   | All Node components      |
| `Links`                                          | `ObservableCollection<IWorkflowLinkViewModel>`                                                   | All Link components      |
| `Layout`                                         | `CanvasLayout`                                                                                   | Canvas layout            |

| Member                                             | Type                                                                                             | Description              |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------ |
| `CreateNodeCommand`                              | `IVeloxCommand`                                                                                  | Create node command      |
| `SetPointerCommand`                              | `IVeloxCommand`                                                                                  | Set pointer position command |
| `ResetVirtualLinkCommand`                        | `IVeloxCommand`                                                                                  | Reset virtual link command |
| `RedoCommand`                                    | `IVeloxCommand`                                                                                  | Redo command             |
| `UndoCommand`                                    | `IVeloxCommand`                                                                                  | Undo command             |

In the definition phase of a node view model, there are two special members:  
- A class derived from `IWorkflowSlotViewModel`, which must be annotated with `[VeloxProperty]`.  
- A `SlotEnumerator<T>`, which can use `bool` or `enum` as conditional branches; the collection internally generates multiple slots based on the condition and must also be annotated with `[VeloxProperty]`.  

With this setup, you can later directly render multiple connectors in the view using the `InputSlot` property and the `OutputSlots` collection. Note that the UI is responsible for tracking slot coordinates (anchors) and passing them back to the view model. If the UI lacks such coordinate tracking, the start and end positions of your `Link` component cannot be computed correctly.

The `SlotEnumerator<T>` implements the `IEnumerable` interface, with the following child elements defined:  
- `Name`: the name of the enumeration member  
- `Value`: the enumeration value  
- `Slot`: the automatically constructed `Slot` component

Children.Code:
public partial class ConditionalSlot<TSlot>
    where TSlot : IWorkflowSlotViewModel, new()
{
    [VeloxProperty] private string _name = string.Empty; // Name of the conditional slot
    [VeloxProperty] private object? _value; // Current value of the conditional slot
    [VeloxProperty] private TSlot _slot = new(); // Associated workflow slot view model instance
}

The logic of all workflow components is injectable and replaceable, and events and overridable methods are provided to allow customization. Each workflow component you define holds a `Helper` property, through which you can access the business implementation.
After using the generic version, all built-in Component properties of Helper will become concrete derived classes; in the non-generic version, the Component property is in interface abstract form.

Cells: Provides extended behaviors for the global container
Provides extended behaviors for connection lines

If you have already read the sections on component construction and business logic injection, the workflow is now ready to run.
This chapter addresses the following question: How to implement a workflow GUI based on attached properties.

This section applies to Avalonia / WPF / WinUI / MAUI (WinForms has no attached property mechanism—its API is merely similar; the recommended practice is to directly port from the repository’s demo; Razor is still experimental).

Children.Code: <UserControl xmlns:behaviors="using:VeloxDev.WorkflowSystem.AttachedBehaviors" />

Children.Text: The repository contains two Avalonia samples, one of which has been manually trimmed to retain the minimal code template and has successfully run on Android / Browser / Desktop. You can click the links below to view them.
Provides behavior registration based on Name at the UserControl level, covering interactive behaviors required by elements such as Canvas, ScrollViewer, and grid adornment lines.

| Additional Property | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `IsEnabled` | `bool` | Enables the entire workflow canvas interaction behavior |
| `ScrollViewerName` | `string?` | Specifies the `ScrollViewer` used for scrolling and viewport calculations |
| `CanvasName` | `string?` | Specifies the workflow content canvas |
| `GridDecoratorName` | `string?` | Specifies the grid/ruler decorator used to synchronize scroll offset and content offset |
| `PointerPressSourceName` | `string?` | Specifies the element that receives pointer press events and initiates canvas panning |
Provides an object pooling mechanism for Canvas; reusing view elements plays a critical role in reducing GC pressure and improving rendering performance.

Cells: Interface for GUI framework-specific template selector

Children.Code:
<UserControl xmlns="https://github.com/avaloniaui"
			 xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2006/xaml"
			 xmlns:d="http://schemas.microsoft.com/expression/blend/2008"
			 xmlns:mc="http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/markup-compatibility/2006"
			 mc:Ignorable="d" d:DesignWidth="800" d:DesignHeight="450"
			 x:Class="Demo.WorkflowView"
			 xmlns:local="using:Demo"
			 xmlns:vm="using:Demo.ViewModels"
			 xmlns:workflow="using:VeloxDev.WorkflowSystem"
			 x:DataType="vm:TreeViewModel"

             xmlns:behaviors="using:VeloxDev.WorkflowSystem.AttachedBehaviors"
             behaviors:WorkflowSurfaceBehavior.IsEnabled="True"
             behaviors:WorkflowSurfaceBehavior.ScrollViewerName="PART_ScrollViewer"
			 behaviors:WorkflowSurfaceBehavior.CanvasName="PART_Canvas"
			 behaviors:WorkflowSurfaceBehavior.GridDecoratorName="PART_GridDecorator"
			 behaviors:WorkflowSurfaceBehavior.PointerPressSourceName="PART_SurfaceBorder" >

    <!-- Avalonia automatically matches templates placed here; no need to use ViewPool.TemplateSelector -->
    <!-- Whether RenderTransform needs binding depends on the specific GUI framework -->
    <UserControl.DataTemplates>
        <!-- Node template -->
		<DataTemplate DataType="vm:NodeViewModel">
			<local:NodeView Width="{Binding Size.Width}" Height="{Binding Size.Height}"
							Canvas.Left="{Binding Anchor.Horizontal}" Canvas.Top="{Binding Anchor.Vertical}" ZIndex="{Binding Anchor.Layer}"
							RenderTransform="{Binding $parent[local:WorkflowView].(behaviors:WorkflowCanvasTransformBehavior.Transform)}"/>
		</DataTemplate>

        <!-- Connection template -->
		<DataTemplate DataType="vm:LinkViewModel">
			<local:BezierCurveView StartLeft="{Binding Sender.Anchor.Horizontal}" StartTop="{Binding Sender.Anchor.Vertical}"
								   EndLeft="{Binding Receiver.Anchor.Horizontal}" EndTop="{Binding Receiver.Anchor.Vertical}"
                                   ZIndex="-1"
								   CanRender="{Binding IsVisible}"
								   Width="{Binding $parent[Canvas].Bounds.Width}" Height="{Binding $parent[Canvas].Bounds.Height}"
								   RenderTransform="{Binding $parent[local:WorkflowView].(behaviors:WorkflowCanvasTransformBehavior.Transform)}" />
		</DataTemplate>
    </UserControl.DataTemplates>

	<!-- Render node editor (with virtualization support) -->
	<Grid Grid.Column="2">
        <Border x:Name="PART_SurfaceBorder" BorderBrush="White" BorderThickness="1" CornerRadius="3">
            <local:WorkflowGridDecorator x:Name="PART_GridDecorator">
                <ScrollViewer x:Name="PART_ScrollViewer" HorizontalScrollBarVisibility="Auto" >
				    <Canvas x:Name="PART_Canvas"
                            x:DataType="vm:TreeViewModel" Width="{Binding Layout.ActualSize.Width}" Height="{Binding Layout.ActualSize.Height}" HorizontalAlignment="Left" VerticalAlignment="Top"
                            behaviors:ViewPool.ItemsSource="{Binding Helper.VisibleItems}">
					    <Canvas.DataTemplates>
						    <!-- Data templates are searched in order of priority: Self > Parent > App -->
					    </Canvas.DataTemplates>
				    </Canvas>
			    </ScrollViewer>
		    </local:WorkflowGridDecorator>
	    </Border>
	</Grid>

</UserControl>

Children.Text: Node mount connectors — this set of additional attributes governs the behavior of these connectors.

Cells: Slot must be deployed in a hosting container
Drag-and-drop will perform coordinate detection based on a host container

Children.Code:
<UserControl xmlns="https://github.com/avaloniaui"
			 xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2006/xaml"
			 xmlns:d="http://schemas.microsoft.com/expression/blend/2008"
			 xmlns:mc="http://schemas.openxmlformats.org/markup-compatibility/2006"
			 xmlns:vm="using:Demo.ViewModels.Workflow"
			 xmlns:local="using:Demo"
			 mc:Ignorable="d" d:DesignWidth="800" d:DesignHeight="450"
			 x:Class="Demo.NodeView"
			 x:DataType="vm:NodeViewModel"
			 xmlns:behaviors="using:VeloxDev.WorkflowSystem.AttachedBehaviors"
			 behaviors:WorkflowSlotLayoutBehavior.IsEnabled="True"
			 behaviors:WorkflowSlotLayoutBehavior.SlotNames="PART_InputSlot"
			 behaviors:WorkflowSlotLayoutBehavior.SlotEnumeratorNames="PART_OutputSlots"
			 behaviors:WorkflowSlotLayoutBehavior.CoordinateHostName="PART_Canvas" >

	<Grid RowDefinitions="48,*">
		<!-- Title -->
		<Border Grid.Row="0"
				Background="#3a2550"
				CornerRadius="4,4,0,0"
				BorderThickness="0,0,0,0.5"
				BorderBrush="#d6a0ff"
				behaviors:WorkflowNodeDragBehavior.CoordinateHostName="PART_Canvas"
				behaviors:WorkflowNodeDragBehavior.IsEnabled="True">
			<StackPanel Orientation="Horizontal" HorizontalAlignment="Center" VerticalAlignment="Center" Spacing="8">
				<TextBlock Text="{Binding Name}" Foreground="White" FontWeight="Bold"/>
			</StackPanel>
		</Border>

		<!-- Connector: input port -->
		<Grid Grid.Row="1" ZIndex="6">
			<local:SlotView x:Name="PART_InputSlot"
							Width="20" Height="20"
							HorizontalAlignment="Left"
							VerticalAlignment="Center"
							DataContext="{Binding InputSlot}" />
		</Grid>

		<!-- Connectors: multiple output ports auto-generated from the enumeration -->
		<Border Grid.Row="1" Background="#2a1e35" CornerRadius="0,0,4,4">
			<ItemsControl x:Name="PART_OutputSlots"
						  ItemsSource="{Binding OutputSlots.Items}"
						  VerticalAlignment="Center" >
				<ItemsControl.ItemTemplate>
					<DataTemplate>
						<Grid ColumnDefinitions="*,Auto" Margin="0,2" ClipToBounds="False">
							<TextBlock Grid.Column="0"
									   Text="{Binding Name}"
									   Foreground="#d6a0ff"
									   FontWeight="SemiBold"
									   VerticalAlignment="Center"
									   HorizontalAlignment="Right"
									   Margin="0,0,16,0"/>
							<local:SlotView Grid.Column="1" Width="16" Height="16"
											HorizontalAlignment="Right"
											VerticalAlignment="Center"
											DataContext="{Binding Slot}" />
						</Grid>
					</DataTemplate>
				</ItemsControl.ItemTemplate>
			</ItemsControl>
		</Border>
	</Grid>

</UserControl>

VeloxDev.Core.Extension provides a set of extension methods for view models, with the following features:  
- `Dictionary<,>` supports any type as the key, no longer limited to `string`.  
- Only properties whose setter and getter are both `public` will be serialized, and serialization handles only properties.

| Target / Scenario | Configuration Syntax | Why This Configuration | Recommended? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Debugging, viewing JSON, performing diffs | `SerializationOptions.Create().WithIndented().WithTypeNameHandling(TypeNameHandling.Auto).WithNullValueHandling(NullValueHandling.Include).WithDefaultValueHandling(DefaultValueHandling.Include)` | Maximizes JSON readability and fully preserves polymorphism, null values, and default values — ideal for diagnosing workflow restoration issues | Strongly recommended |
| Production saving to file | `SerializationOptions.Create().WithCompact().WithTypeNameHandling(TypeNameHandling.Auto).WithNullValueHandling(NullValueHandling.Include).WithDefaultValueHandling(DefaultValueHandling.Include)` | Produces smaller files while retaining all information required for full workflow persistence and restoration | Recommended |
| Minimal JSON size only | `SerializationOptions.Create().WithCompact().WithNullValueHandling(NullValueHandling.Ignore).WithDefaultValueHandling(DefaultValueHandling.Ignore)` | Reduces JSON size but may omit fields essential for restoration — unsuitable for complex workflow object graphs | Use with caution |
| Derived nodes, interface properties, or polymorphic objects | `WithTypeNameHandling(TypeNameHandling.Auto)` | Enables deserialization to reconstruct the actual runtime type; typically required in Workflow scenarios | Required |
| Exact state restoration after deserialization | `WithNullValueHandling(NullValueHandling.Include).WithDefaultValueHandling(DefaultValueHandling.Include)` | Preserves `null` and default values to prevent state drift post-deserialization | Recommended |
| Improved JSON readability only | `WithIndented()` | Improves human readability only; does not alter object semantics | As needed |
| Reduced file size only | `WithCompact()` | Removes indentation and whitespace — simplest form of compression | As needed |
| Synchronous API usage | `var json = tree.Serialize(options);` / `var tree = json.Deserialize<TreeViewModel>(options);` | Suitable for simple save/load operations | Recommended |
| Safe loading (e.g., from untrusted sources) | `json.TryDeserialize<TreeViewModel>(options, out var tree)` | More robust when loading from potentially corrupted external files or user input — avoids throwing exceptions | Strongly recommended |
| Asynchronous API usage | `await tree.SerializeAsync(options)` / `await json.DeserializeAsync<TreeViewModel>(options)` | Suitable for UI, CLI commands, and file I/O pipelines | Recommended |
| Stream-based API usage | `await tree.SerializeToStreamAsync(stream, options)` / `await stream.DeserializeFromStreamAsync<TreeViewModel>(options)` | Suitable for file streams, network streams, and memory streams | Recommended |
| Most robust default for Workflows | `SerializationOptions.Create().WithIndented().WithTypeNameHandling(TypeNameHandling.Auto).WithNullValueHandling(NullValueHandling.Include).WithDefaultValueHandling(DefaultValueHandling.Include)` | Use this if uncertain — safest for workflow object graphs | Default choice |
This section continuously tracks the usage of VeloxDev across different GUI frameworks, gathering and addressing questions.
To ensure thread safety, when using VeloxDev.WinUI, you must let UIThreadInspector obtain the window.

VeloxDev.Core.Extension provides serialization that depends on reflection, and some agent capabilities rely on serialization. If you encounter issues where the Agent fails to run or serialization fails in a trimming-sensitive environment, try disabling trimming.

Children.Code: <PublishTrimmed>false</PublishTrimmed>

Children.Text: Because not all runtimes support `Thread`, the frame loop follows two paths. Conservatively, versions earlier than .NET 5 default to using asynchronous execution instead of `Thread`; later versions have built-in platform detection, and their detection logic follows the table below.

The MonoBehaviourManager class has a static property named `UseAsyncLoop`. When set to `true`, it enables asynchronous execution for maximum compatibility. You can modify `UseAsyncLoop` before calling the `Start()` method provided by this class.

Check your component mounting order: the `Node` component must be mounted to the `Tree` component before executing its internal commands, and the `Slot` component must be mounted to the `Node` component before executing its internal commands.
In our plan, we hope to collect some best practices—feel free to submit any interesting projects you’ve built with VeloxDev to the email address below.
